title: Cat Swollen and Hot Mammary Glands: Causes and Emergency Care
meta_desc: If your cat has swollen, hot mammary glands, it could be mastitis or a serious infection. Learn why high urgency care and veterinary triage are needed.
content_html:
What is it?

Swollen, hot, and painful mammary glands in cats are most often a sign of mastitis, an infection of the breast tissue. This condition is common in nursing mothers but can occasionally occur in non-pregnant cats. Other possibilities include feline mammary hypertrophy (rapid benign growth) or mammary tumors, though heat and sudden swelling typically point toward an inflammatory or infectious process.

Is it an Emergency?

Urgency Level: High. This condition should be treated as an emergency. Mastitis can quickly progress to systemic infection or sepsis, which is life-threatening for the mother. If the glands are turning purple, black, or if your cat is lethargic and refusing to eat, she needs to see a veterinarian immediately to prevent the infection from spreading to her bloodstream.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

Providing a clear, well-lit photo of the affected area can be incredibly helpful for a veterinary professional. It allows us to assess the severity of the inflammation, look for signs of tissue necrosis (skin death), or identify any abnormal discharge. A visual check helps determine if your cat needs immediate surgical intervention or if stabilization with IV fluids and antibiotics is the first priority.

faqs.question: Can I treat my cat's swollen mammary glands at home?
faqs.answer: No, home treatment is not recommended. Mastitis and mammary infections require professional diagnosis and often involve prescription antibiotics or pain management that cannot be safely administered without a vet.

faqs.question: Is mastitis painful for my cat?
faqs.answer: Yes, mastitis is typically very painful. Your cat may become aggressive when her belly is touched or may avoid nursing her kittens because of the discomfort.

faqs.question: Should I let the kittens continue to nurse?
faqs.answer: You should consult a vet before allowing nursing to continue. If the milk is infected or contains bacteria/pus, it can make the kittens extremely ill.
